commercialization_path.acquirer_value: A model lab, AI platform, or security vendor gets a control surface for remote-agent discovery before A2A delegation and MCP tool access become production defaults.
commercialization_path.enterprise_layer: Sell hosted Agent Card monitoring, signature verification, allowlist drift detection, procurement exports, A2A gateway policy, and tenant-specific remote-agent trust tiers.
commercialization_path.open_layer: Publish Agent Card trust evaluation as open evidence and a local CLI so A2A pilots can start with a shared security vocabulary.

enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes treats remote-agent discovery as a trust decision before context, authority, or workflow evidence moves across A2A.
default_questions_answered: Is the Agent Card complete enough for enterprise review?
Does the card advertise HTTPS interfaces and provider identity?
Does the card require standard HTTP-layer authentication?
Is the card signed or restricted to metadata-only pilot use?
Do advertised skills require approval, gateway enforcement, or hard denial?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Run this profile during A2A pilot intake, remote-agent procurement review, inter-agent handoff design, and MCP gateway trust-tier promotion.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Lead with open Agent Card evaluation, then sell hosted Agent Card monitoring, signature verification, allowlist drift detection, tenant policy exports, and A2A/MCP gateway enforcement.

a2a_protocol_surface.description: An agent delegates a task to an opaque remote agent through HTTP and JSON-RPC style task exchange.
a2a_protocol_surface.title: A2A task-delegation boundary

intake_profiles.description: Use when a remote A2A agent can receive cited evidence or approval-gated context after signed-card validation, provider identity review, HTTP-layer auth, scope minimization, and gateway enforcement.
intake_profiles.title: Trusted Production A2A Agent

intake_profiles.description: Use when the card is structurally valid and authenticated, but lacks full production evidence. The agent may receive metadata-only context while signatures, scopes, and ownership are completed.
intake_profiles.title: Restricted Pilot A2A Agent

intake_profiles.description: Use for unauthenticated public Agent Cards that may be indexed or reviewed but must not receive tenant context, workflow evidence, or delegated authority.
intake_profiles.title: Public Discovery Only

intake_profiles.description: Use when a card leaks secrets, advertises insecure interfaces, requests dangerous skill authority without controls, or includes prompt-injection instructions.
intake_profiles.title: Blocked or Malicious Agent Card

intent: Define the SecurityRecipes A2A Agent Card trust profile so remote agents can be discovered, scored, piloted, denied, or killed before they receive secure context or join an agent-to-agent workflow. The profile turns an A2A Agent Card into enterprise intake evidence for protocol identity, HTTPS interfaces, authentication, authorization scope, signing, skill risk, extended-card handling, and prompt-injection containment.

positioning.buyer: AI Platform, Product Security, AppSec, IAM, GRC, procurement security, model-risk, and acquisition diligence teams
positioning.category: A2A remote-agent discovery and Agent Card trust intake
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes can evaluate an A2A Agent Card before trust is granted, return a deterministic allow, pilot, hold, deny, or kill decision, and expose the result through generated JSON, CLI, CI, and MCP tools.
positioning.why_now: A2A makes remote agents discoverable through Agent Cards while MCP standardizes tool access. Enterprises need a trust layer between discovery and delegation so signed cards, HTTP-layer auth, skill scope, provider identity, and safe context release are validated before opaque agents collaborate.

residual_risks.risk: The open evaluator checks Agent Card evidence but does not cryptographically verify signatures.
residual_risks.treatment: Production deployments should verify JWS signatures against tenant or vendor trust roots before promoting an agent to the trusted-production profile.

residual_risks.risk: An Agent Card can describe safe behavior while the remote opaque agent behaves differently at runtime.
residual_risks.treatment: Bind Agent Card trust to gateway telemetry, handoff receipts, eval replay, output inspection, and per-skill allowlists.

residual_risks.risk: A2A and MCP adoption can compose into multi-hop chains where one trusted card delegates to another untrusted agent.
residual_risks.treatment: Require a fresh Agent Card trust decision and handoff decision at every hop, with correlation IDs propagated across the chain.

sample_agent_card_evaluations.reason: Agent Card satisfies production profile requirements
Agent Card is eligible only for restricted metadata-context pilot use
Agent Card contains credential, hidden-instruction, or exfiltration markers
violations: agent card contains prohibited marker: access_token=
agent card contains prohibited marker: bypass approval
agent card contains prohibited marker: exfiltrate
agent card contains prohibited marker: ignore previous instructions
agent card contains prohibited marker: sk-

standards_alignment.coverage: Agent Card fields, supported interfaces, security schemes, security requirements, signatures, authenticated extended cards, and the .well-known/agent-card.json discovery surface.
standards_alignment.name: Agent2Agent Protocol Specification

standards_alignment.coverage: HTTPS, TLS, server identity verification, HTTP-layer authentication, authorization, data privacy, tracing, monitoring, API management, and governance for remote opaque agents.
standards_alignment.name: A2A Enterprise Implementation Guidance

standards_alignment.coverage: Enterprise A2A adoption signals including signed security cards, gRPC support, and developer tooling for building, deploying, evaluating, and selling A2A agents.
standards_alignment.name: Google Cloud A2A Enterprise Toolkit

standards_alignment.coverage: Complements A2A by keeping tool access behind resource indicators, token audience validation, protected resource metadata, PKCE, and token-passthrough denial.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization Specification

standards_alignment.coverage: Anchors the Agent Card trust model in source-sink analysis, constrained impact, user confirmation for sensitive transmissions, and defense against social-engineering style prompt injection.
standards_alignment.name: OpenAI: Designing AI Agents to Resist Prompt Injection

standards_alignment.coverage: Maps remote-agent intake to goal hijacking, tool misuse, identity abuse, insecure inter-agent communication, memory/context poisoning, and cascading failures.
standards_alignment.name: OWASP Agentic AI Threats and Mitigations

standards_alignment.coverage: Supports vendor-neutral AI control objectives, AI-CAIQ style evidence, role accountability, cloud AI governance, and third-party AI service assessment.
standards_alignment.name: CSA AI Controls Matrix

threat_signal_coverage.title: Agent identities are becoming a new non-human identity class
Agent handoffs are becoming a protocol trust boundary
AI data security guidance is converging on provenance, integrity, and monitoring
Context over-sharing and memory poisoning make retrieval policy a product category
Human approval is shifting from UX prompt to policy-enforced control
Indirect prompt injection is now social engineering against agents
MCP token audience, passthrough, and scope creep are control-plane risks

decisions.meaning: The card satisfies production trust requirements for HTTPS interface URLs, provider identity, authentication, least-privilege scopes, signatures, safe skills, and required controls.
The card is structurally valid and authenticated but lacks full production evidence, so it may receive metadata-only or public context during a restricted pilot.
The card is incomplete, unsigned, over-broad, or missing declared controls that a human owner or gateway policy must review before use.
The card uses insecure transport, unsafe API key placement, missing authentication for production, unsupported interfaces, or risky skill declarations that should not be piloted.
The card appears to contain credential material, tokens, private keys, seed phrases, hidden prompts, or direct exfiltration instructions.
